attributes.stem:
In the field of medical diagnostics, computed tomography (CT) scanning plays a crucial role as an imaging modality, offering a distinctive perspective on internal structures. In comparison to other imaging techniques such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), ultrasound (US), plain X-rays, and positron emission tomography (PET), CT scans are widely utilised in modern healthcare. With the increasing role of radiology in today's medicine, attention must be paid to amount of radiation being delivered to patients and the associated risks. Whilst the exact number does not need to be recalled in every case, it is important to discuss potential radiation risks with patients and if there are benefits of the investigation that outweigh the risks.

The CT scan below is an example of a part of a triple-phase CT scan of the abdomen (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: The effective dose of this particular investigation is similar to approximately how many chest x-rays?
attributes.explanation:
An individual will be exposed to about 2 - 3 mSv (millisieverts) of background radiation a year. In the United States of America, the proportion of total per capita radiation exposure from medical imaging has increased from about 15% in the 1980s to about 50% in 2020. In the 1980s there were about 3 million CTs performed annually - and that has now increased to about 375 million a year. CT scanning in Australia showed a significant increase from 4,663.5 per 100,000 person-years in 2001 to 14,506 in 2019 (211% increase). Some 25% of all radiation exposure comes from CT scans. The ALARA principle of as low as reasonably achievable should be applied for any potential investigations.

A single chest PA only X-ray delivers an effective dose of about 0.02 - 0.1mSv of radiation, whilst a chest CT delivers approximately an effective dose of 6 - 8 mSv. Abdomen and pelvis CT deliver approximately an effective dose of about 8 - 10 mSv. The CT given in the stem is a triple-phase CT scan of the abdomen, which would expose the patient to repeated radiation doses and the approximate effective radiation dose is approximately 5 - 15mSv, equivalent to at least 150 chest X-rays, or equivalent to 5 years of natural background radiation. A CT angiogram delivers approximately 9 mSv. Because the data is extracted from modelling software rather than a per-patient basis, the effective dose of radiation is approximate but the rough estimates can still be determined.

These CT radiation doses are very large - and as such are not always appreciated by medical practitioners, let alone the patients. Whilst few can doubt the enormous potential benefits of imaging in today's healthcare environment, all too often the hazards associated with these technologies are forgotten. Over-exposure to high doses of radiation is a real risk and a potential source of malignancy - particularly when CT scans and similar imaging studies are used in childhood. New CT scanners are appearing that are associated with reduced radiation exposure - but still with dosage sufficiently large that practitioners should always ask if the investigation is really necessary. For example, treatment with radiotherapy for bowel cancer may later cause melanoma elsewhere secondary to the radiotherapy.

CT technology has undergone great advancements over the years, leading to improvements in image quality, diagnostic accuracy, and most significantly, a reduction in the amount of radiation delivered to patients. Iterative reconstruction algorithms and automatic exposure control help to reduce the radiation dose given and retain the quality of the image. Additionally, photon-counting CT represents a cutting-edge innovation in the field, revolutionising the way X-ray photons are detected and contributing to further improvements in image quality, material differentiation, and radiation dose reduction.

The risk of haematological malignancies after CT scanning is significantly increased. A multinational European study of a million young people who had undergone at least one CT scan before the age of 22 found that the subsequent risk of haematological malignancies increased in proportion to the dose of ionising radiation received. A multinational European study of a million people under 22 years of age who had one CT scan in their lifetime demonstrated that the risk was increased by about 16% for those undergoing a typical 8 mSv scan. That is, for a twelve year forecast, for every 10, 000 children who have a CT scan, one or two will develop a haematological malignancy due to the investigation.
learningPoints: A triple-phase CT scan of the abdomen delivers a radiation dose roughly equivalent to that of 150 chest X-rays.
references: RadiologyInfo.org. Radiation Dose [Internet]. Radiological Society of North America; [updated 2022 Nov 1]. Available from: https://www.radiologyinfo.org/en/info/safety-xray
Imaging Technology News. Advancements in Computed Tomography Technology [Internet]. 2023 Nov 1. Available from: https://www.itnonline.com/article/advancements-computed-tomography-technology

Graafen D, Emrich T, Halfmann MC, Mildenberger P, Düber C, Yang Y, Othman AE, O' Doherty J, Müller L, Kloeckner R. Dose Reduction and Image Quality in Photon-counting Detector High-resolution Computed Tomography of the Chest: Routine Clinical Data. J Thorac Imaging. 2022 Sep 1;37(5):315-322. doi: 10.1097/RTI.0000000000000661. Epub 2022 Jun 14. PMID: 35699680.
Bosch de Basea Gomez M, Thierry-Chef I, Harbron R, et al. Risk of hematological malignancies from CT radiation exposure in children, adolescents and young adults. Nat Med 2023; 29:3111–19
